does: This paper introduces Dr. Post-Training, a framework that reinterprets post-training of large language models through the lens of data regularization. Rather than treating general training data as a source pool for selection, the method constructs a feasible set of model update directions from general data at each training step and projects target-data gradients onto this feasible set. This prevents overfitting to scarce, high-quality target objectives while maintaining performance on broader capabilities. The framework shows that standard training and existing data selection methods emerge as special cases corresponding to different points on a bias-variance spectrum, with varying regularization strengths. The approach enables systematic design of new post-training methods with principled trade-offs between target alignment and general capability preservation.
connects: This work builds on recent research in data selection for LLM post-training, which has sought to efficiently use limited high-quality instruction data alongside abundant general pretraining data. It extends prior work that frames post-training as a multi-objective optimization problem, connecting to classical regularization theory and constrained optimization. The projection-based approach relates to earlier methods in continual learning and catastrophic forgetting that similarly balance new task learning against retention of prior knowledge. The bias-variance perspective connects to foundational statistical learning theory, providing a unifying lens across supervised fine-tuning, instruction tuning, and data selection approaches that have been developed somewhat independently in recent LLM literature.
enables: This regularization-based framework opens opportunities for developing new post-training methods with explicit control over the generalization-specialization trade-off. Future work can leverage the feasible-set construction to design adaptive regularization schemes that vary across training stages or layers, and to theoretically characterize optimal regularization strength given data budgets. The perspective enables more principled investigation of data mixtures for post-training, potentially reducing the empirical tuning required in current approaches. It also suggests extensions to multi-task or multi-objective post-training scenarios, and could inform curriculum-based strategies where regularization strength changes as target model performance improves.
